---
title: "Waypoint Navigation in ArduRover (Boats)"
subtitle: "A conceptual model — how a mission of waypoints becomes throttle and steering commands"
---

[← Back to Autopilot](index.qmd)

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
This page is a **conceptual overview** for students using ArduRover on the USV. It is intentionally light on algorithm internals. Detailed analysis of guidance and navigation (G&N) is **beyond the scope of this course**; the goal here is just enough mental scaffolding to predict and explain mission behavior, with pointers outward when you want the real depth.
:::

## What a waypoint mission is

- A **mission** is an ordered list of (lat, lon, alt) targets uploaded to the autopilot.
- In **AUTO** mode the autopilot walks the list, treating each entry as the current target until "arrived", then advances.
- For a USV the only relevant target is the (lat, lon) pair; altitude is ignored.

## Where waypoint navigation sits in the stack

- Outermost loop in the same three-layer cascade students saw in [Lab 2](../weeks/w07_lab_usv_pid/index.qmd).
- Above: pilot / Mission Planner sets up the mission.
- Below: the **stabilization layer** ([reference](stabilization.qmd)) — the two PID + feedforward loops on speed and yaw-rate that you tuned in Lab 2.
- The boundary that matters: the navigation layer produces the **same `THR_DesSpeed` and `STER_DesTurnRate` setpoints** that the stabilization layer was already chasing in Lab 2. Nothing changes downstream.

## The conceptual chain, step by step

1. **Active waypoint.** Pick the next item in the mission list. This is the navigation-level setpoint.
2. **Geometry.** Compute the vector from current (GPS / EKF) position to the active waypoint — yields a desired bearing and a remaining distance.
3. **Cross-track error.** Decompose the position error relative to the *planned path* (a line from the previous waypoint to the active one) into along-track and across-track components. The cross-track component is what gets pushed against to keep the boat on the line.
4. **Desired speed.** Capped above by `WP_SPEED` (your "cruise" setting); capped from below by what the kinematic limits (`ATC_ACCEL_MAX`, `ATC_DECEL_MAX`, `ATC_TURN_MAX_G`) allow at this point on the path. The boat slows down in tight corners on purpose.
5. **Desired yaw rate.** Computed so the boat steers toward the bearing while respecting the turning g-cap. This includes a proportional response on cross-track error.
6. **Setpoint handoff (the bridge to Lab 2).**
    - Desired speed → `THR_DesSpeed` → speed PID
    - Desired yaw rate → `STER_DesTurnRate` → yaw-rate PID
7. **Arrival check.** "Am I within `WP_RADIUS` of the active waypoint?" If yes, advance to the next waypoint. If this was the last waypoint, the mission is complete.

## The PID analogy

The navigation layer is structured like the loops you already tuned — same pattern, one level up.

| Concept | Lab 2 stabilization | Navigation layer |
|---|---|---|
| **Setpoint** | speed (m/s), yaw-rate (rad/s) | active waypoint (lat, lon) |
| **Measurement** | `GPS_Spd`, EKF yaw rate | EKF position |
| **Error** | speed − measured speed | cross-track + along-track distance |
| **Proportional gain** | `ATC_SPEED_P`, `ATC_STR_RAT_P` | implicit, inside `AC_PosControl` (parameters `PSC_*`, fixed across teams) |
| **Feedforward** | `ATC_*_FF × setpoint` (Lab 1's `1/K_dc`) | S-curve velocity reference — kinematically feasible by construction |
| **Output** | throttle PWM, rudder PWM | `THR_DesSpeed`, `STER_DesTurnRate` (= next layer's setpoint) |

: {tbl-colwidths="[20, 38, 42]"}

Two takeaways:

- **Same internal pattern.** Setpoint → error → P/I/D → output, with an optional feedforward branch that supplies an open-loop guess.
- **Recursive cascade.** Each layer's output is the next layer's setpoint. Waypoint mission execution is just the Lab-2 block diagram nested inside one more wrapper.

## What students should think about (and what to leave alone)

**Worth knowing — these dominate mission performance:**

- `WP_SPEED` — cruise speed along the path (m/s).
- `WP_RADIUS` — how close counts as "arrived".
- `WP_OVERSHOOT` — how much lateral overshoot is tolerated when cutting corners.
- `ATC_ACCEL_MAX`, `ATC_DECEL_MAX`, `ATC_TURN_MAX_G` — the kinematic caps; these constrain what the navigation layer is allowed to ask for.
- That **cross-track error exists** and is what pulls the boat back onto the path when wind / current push it off.

**Out of scope for this course:**

- S-curve internals (jerk-limited 7-segment trajectory generator).
- `PSC_*` position-controller PID gains.
- L1 navigation algorithm derivation, NPFG, spline blending.
- EKF / state estimation.

These shape the behavior you see in the logs, but you are not expected to tune or analyze them.

## External references

**Conceptual + parameter handbook (Rover-specific):**

- [ArduRover → Navigation](https://ardupilot.org/rover/docs/navigation.html) — what the autopilot does in AUTO mode; mission semantics.
- [ArduRover → Position Control](https://ardupilot.org/rover/docs/position-control.html) — how the boat is steered toward a target.
- [ArduRover → Tuning Navigation](https://ardupilot.org/rover/docs/rover-tuning-navigation.html) — per-parameter handbook for `WP_*`, `PSC_*`, the S-curve caps.
- [ArduRover → AUTO Mode](https://ardupilot.org/rover/docs/auto-mode.html) — mission upload, mode switching, behavior on mission end.

**Algorithm internals (out of scope, but the canonical sources if you go looking):**

- [Copter Position Control and Navigation — dev wiki](https://ardupilot.org/dev/docs/code-overview-copter-poscontrol-and-navigation.html) — Rover uses the same `AC_WPNav` / `AC_PosControl` / `SCurve` libraries the wiki describes; despite the "Copter" title it's the right architecture doc.
- [Trigonometric S-Curve Navigation — ArduPilot UnConference 2019 (Leonard Hall)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LHq5o9zgNWk) — ~30 min, closest thing to a paper on the trajectory generator.
- [Leash vs S-Curve navigation — ArduPilot Discourse](https://discuss.ardupilot.org/t/leash-vs-s-curve-navigation/64745) — before/after comparison.
- [`AC_WPNav.h`](https://github.com/ArduPilot/ardupilot/blob/master/libraries/AC_WPNav/AC_WPNav.h), [`SCurve.h`](https://github.com/ArduPilot/ardupilot/blob/master/libraries/AP_Math/SCurve.h) — source headers, more candid than the user wiki.

**Within this course:**

- [Stabilization Layer](stabilization.qmd) — the inner loop the navigation layer hands off to. The mental model on this page connects directly to that one.
- [Lab 3 overview](../weeks/w10_lab_usv_waypoint/index.qmd) — where the conceptual model gets exercised in a field session.
- [Lab 3 G&N companion](../weeks/w10_lab_usv_waypoint/guidance_navigation.qmd) — lab-specific walk through the same cascade with emphasis on the bridge from Lab 2.
